phases.label: Phase 15A
phases.title: Phase 15A: Portable Agam Package and Tiered Runtime Platform
goal: Define one portable Agam package contract and one stable runtime contract that can execute verified MIR across hosts.
Keep portable package execution and native AOT release builds aligned under one product story instead of separate backend silos.
main_motive: Give Agam one portable package/runtime story instead of treating every backend as a separate product.
Make execution feel vertically integrated: one package contract, one runtime ABI, one path into JIT or AOT release outputs.
already_done: Built the first portable package format in `agam_pkg` as a JSON-backed `.agpkg.json` artifact carrying verified MIR, effect metadata, coarse source maps, backend preference, and runtime requirements.
Introduced a stable runtime contract in `agam_runtime::contract` with ABI versioning, host metadata, load validation, and package load planning so tools share one package/runtime manifest shape.
Added serializable MIR support for the current package flow and wired `agamc package pack`, `agamc package inspect`, and `agamc package run` in `agam_driver`.
Portable package execution now loads through `agam_runtime` and tiers directly into the Cranelift JIT.
Existing LLVM and C native AOT paths remain the release build surface under `agamc build`.
what_to_build_next: Treat this phase as foundation already shipped.
Build on it through cache, daemon, SDK, and agent-execution work rather than reopening the package contract casually.
agent_interpretation: Java-like portability for Agam means portable package plus Agam runtime, not one identical native binary on every OS.
This phase is the foundation for caching, daemon work, SDK distribution, and future agent-facing execution products.

phases.label: Phase 15B
phases.title: Phase 15B: Persistent Native and Semantic Cache
goal: Persist semantically verified package and native build artifacts across runs.
Make repeated packaging and native builds restore from bounded, inspectable caches instead of recompiling identical work.
main_motive: Stop recompiling identical validated work across runs.
Make repeated builds and package flows feel fast because the compiler remembers verified semantics and native outputs.
already_done: Added a repo-local persistent cache under `.agam_cache/` with JSON metadata plus bounded artifact storage.
Keyed cache entries by source/package hash, semantic hash, backend, runtime ABI, host OS/arch, SIMD tier, optimization level, and feature signature.
Wired portable package caching into `agamc package pack`.
Wired native build caching into `agamc build` so repeated C/LLVM builds can restore cached output artifacts instead of recompiling.
Kept the cache version-aware, inspectable, and bounded via schema/compiler/runtime keying plus eviction on entry-count and total-byte limits.
what_to_build_next: Keep later daemon and runtime work cache-aware.
Revisit persistent JIT machine-code caching only when it can fit coherently into the broader cache story.
dependencies_and_prerequisites: Stable cache keys across backend/runtime/version changes
agent_interpretation: The current shipped slice focuses on package artifacts and AOT build outputs.
Persistent JIT machine-code caching was intentionally deferred rather than shipping a half-coherent cache layer.

phases.label: Phase 15C
phases.title: Phase 15C: Whole-Program Purity, Effect Metadata, and Automatic Call Cache
goal: Compute purity and effect facts once at MIR level and reuse them across every backend.
Turn those verified facts into automatic, bounded, and explainable call caching instead of manual hints.
main_motive: Turn purity and side-effect facts into compiler-owned truth instead of manual hints.
Make call caching feel more like ARC: automatic for proven-safe code, bounded, inspectable, and rarely hand-tuned.
already_done: Added shared MIR-level call-cache analysis in `agam_mir::analysis` so purity and effect facts are computed once and reused by both LLVM and JIT backends.
Upgraded automatic call caching to select verified pure scalar functions by default on the current JIT and LLVM paths.
Kept source-level `@lang.feat.no_call_cache` opt-outs and `@experimental.call_cache.optimize` as bounded policy overrides.
Broadened the cache-safe purity model beyond the old "no builtins at all" rule:
allowed stable process-input helpers such as `argc`, `argv`, and `parse_int`
rejected `clock`, stdout writes, opaque pointer state, and unknown or external calls with explicit reasons
Added verbose backend diagnostics so `agamc -v run/build` reports which functions were selected, which were rejected, and why.
what_to_build_next: Build later profiling and specialization work on top of these verified purity and effect facts.
Avoid reintroducing backend-local purity guessing.
dependencies_and_prerequisites: Shared diagnostics and bounded runtime cache policy
agent_interpretation: This phase is what turns later profiling and specialization work into principled optimization rather than guesswork.

phases.label: Phase 15D
phases.title: Phase 15D: Value Profiling, Adaptive Admission, and Specialization
status_notes: Why it mattered now: `info.md` listed this as the first immediate next phase and the remaining open work was still on the critical LLVM/JIT optimization path
goal: Make measured payoff drive cache admission and specialization decisions instead of fixed heuristics.
Keep specialization guarded, reversible, and portable.
main_motive: Make optimization policy follow measured runtime evidence instead of fixed heuristics or hand tuning.
Keep specialization profitable and reversible rather than baking unstable hot-path guesses into the compiler.
checklist_status: [x] Record hot argument shapes, stable scalar values, reuse distance, and real call-cache hit rates at runtime.
[x] Use measured payoff to decide cache admission, specialization cloning, and eviction policy instead of fixed heuristics alone.
[x] Keep specialization guarded and reversible so cold paths remain portable and correct.
already_done: Added `agam_profile` models for adaptive admission decisions, stable scalar value summaries, reuse-distance snapshots, and specialization hints.
Wired JIT call-cache admission to measured payoff signals instead of blindly storing every miss in basic mode.
Extended verbose JIT reporting with unique-key counts, reuse-distance summaries, stable scalar values, and specialization hints.
Added persisted JIT call-cache profiles so later runs can pre-promote hot functions into optimize mode.
Added the first profile-guided specialization cloning slice on the JIT path using persisted stable scalar profiles.
Added the first LLVM-native runtime profile export/import slice through sidecar profile files.
Added specialization-guard hit and fallback tracking to both JIT and LLVM call-cache specialization paths.
Extended persisted profile selection so measured poor specialization match rates suppress future clone plans until later runs produce better evidence.

Extended persisted optimize pre-promotion and specialization retention so favorable specialization guard evidence keeps profitable clones alive while poor match rates stop specialization-only promotions.
Fed specialization guard feedback into live JIT admission payoff and optimized victim scoring so runtime cache policy reacts to measured guarded-clone behavior during the same run.
Mirrored specialization guard feedback weighting on the LLVM optimized cache wrapper so live LLVM admission and replacement scoring also react to measured guarded-clone behavior.

Replaced LLVM's remaining fixed optimized-cache admission gate with an emitted adaptive payoff score based on candidate repeats, reuse distance, hit rate, stable scalar evidence, cache pressure, and specialization feedback.
Added LLVM-side observed unique-key and hottest-key hit tracking so live admission scoring and exported profile imports stop inferring those signals from cache length or store counts.
Tightened LLVM unique-key observation to use a bounded per-function observed-key registry instead of incrementing spread counters on every miss or pending reset.

Switched LLVM optimize-mode admission to use candidate-local observed hit and reuse signals from that bounded registry, and fixed the emitted observed-key scan PHI predecessor so native clang accepts optimized multi-argument cache wrappers.
Mirrored the JIT observed-key retention policy on LLVM by replacing the coldest/oldest observed slot once the bounded registry fills instead of freezing key-shape observation at the first 64 keys.
Mirrored JIT reuse-distance accounting on LLVM by recording observed-key reuse into exported `RD` profile summaries even before a key is admitted into the live cache.

Split LLVM stable-value profiling into vote scores plus exported candidate match counts so `SV` summaries and live stable-slot admission checks track a more faithful dominant-value frequency instead of reusing the Boyer-Moore score directly.

Decoupled persisted guarded specialization from optimize-mode prepromotion so stable-value evidence can keep guarded clones alive on basic cache-enabled paths while optimize promotion remains tied to independent payoff signals such as hit rate, reuse distance, or favorable specialization feedback.

Removed LLVM's remaining optimize-only specialization filter so explicit basic call-cache wrappers can also emit and run guarded specialization clones instead of silently dropping persisted specialization plans outside optimize mode.

Added live guarded-specialization disablement on both JIT and LLVM paths so once same-run specialization feedback becomes clearly unfavorable, later calls bypass clone guards and fall straight back to the generic implementation without continuing to accumulate fallback noise.

Refined live guarded-specialization disablement to track feedback per clone on both JIT and LLVM, so an unfavorable narrow guard can disable itself without shutting off broader guarded clones for the same function during the same run.

Extended the shared/JIT persisted call-cache profile model to retain clone-specific guarded-specialization feedback and use that data when re-planning specializations, so a bad narrow clone no longer suppresses every broader persisted plan for the same function on later JIT runs.

Extended LLVM call-cache profile export/import to schema V6 with clone-specific guarded-specialization feedback lines, so later LLVM runs can reuse the same per-plan suppression and retention logic instead of collapsing everything back to one function-wide specialization score.

Taught both JIT and LLVM backends to honor multiple guarded specialization clones per function, ordered from most-specific to broadest so narrower stable-argument plans are not shadowed by broader ones.
Extended persisted specialization planning to generate progressively broader guarded-clone candidates from the strongest measured stable values instead of only one stable-value bundle per function.
Extended verbose JIT and LLVM reporting with specialization guard hit/fallback summaries.

Closed the remaining live specialization-feedback parity gap by making shared/JIT/LLVM admission and optimized-entry scoring fold aggregate and clone-local guard evidence with the same precedence, so a favorable broader clone now keeps its payoff bonus even when narrower clones go neutral or unfavorable.

Added shared `agam_profile`, JIT, and LLVM regression coverage for clone-local specialization feedback precedence so reversible specialization stays pinned across admission, retention, and emitted wrapper scoring.

Recorded a localized specialization-payoff microbenchmark on a representative two-clone signal mix: old aggregate-only scoring took 64.13 ms across 50,000,000 updates, while the new clone-aware path took 225.04 ms across the same run, which is about 3.22 ns of extra work per score update; the current `bench_call_cache.agam` JIT run with `--call-cache` completed in 39.45 ms on the local dev binary.
Kept the WSL LLVM path development-only via `AGAM_DEV_USE_WSL_LLVM=1`.
Strengthened the native LLVM host-toolchain and SDK story around bundled LLVM, Visual Studio discovery, standard Windows LLVM discovery, and `agamc doctor`.
remaining_work: None in this phase file. Treat Phase 15D as shipped unless later profiling or backend regressions reopen it.
strategic_next_step: Start Phase 15F so parsed/typed/lowered state can stay warm across edits and parallel compilation can begin without losing deterministic diagnostics.
what_to_build_next: Move to Phase 15F.
Keep Phase 15D closed unless new profiling data shows a regression in payoff-driven cache policy or guarded specialization reversibility.
dependencies_and_prerequisites: current JIT profiling and specialization groundwork
native LLVM backend and profile import/export path
agent_interpretation: Treat this phase as complete.
Use it as the optimization-policy baseline for later daemon, package, and SDK work, and only reopen it for measured regressions or genuinely new specialization surfaces.

phases.label: Phase 15E
phases.title: Phase 15E: Escape Analysis + Stack Promotion
status_notes: Strategic note: still listed among immediate-next priorities because it remains an important optimization area
goal: Detect non-escaping values and move them off the heap or scalar-replace them when possible.
Feed stronger alias, lifetime, and value-stability facts into LLVM and JIT paths.
main_motive: Reduce ARC pressure and heap churn by proving short-lived values do not need heap lifetime.
Turn escape and alias facts into backend-visible optimization truth instead of ad hoc assumptions.
checklist_status: [x] Detect non-escaping aggregates and closures for stack placement or scalar replacement.
[x] Feed stronger alias, lifetime, and value-stability facts into LLVM and JIT backends.
[x] Reduce ARC pressure, heap churn, and call-cache key materialization cost on short-lived hot-path values.
already_done: Added `agam_mir::opt::escape` with `NoEscape`, `ArgEscape`, and `GlobalEscape` states.
Added `agam_mir::opt::stack_promote` to mark promotable allocas and track noalias metadata.
Extended MIR instructions with metadata for escape state, stack promotion, and noalias facts.
Integrated escape analysis and stack promotion into the MIR optimization pipeline and driver.
Updated both the C emitter and LLVM emitter to expose stack-promotion and escape annotations.
Verified with 28 MIR unit tests, including 11 new escape-analysis and stack-promotion tests.
what_to_build_next: Treat the core slice as shipped.
Revisit only when later LLVM, daemon, or profiling work exposes a concrete new optimization opportunity.
dependencies_and_prerequisites: backend visibility for stack-promotion and noalias facts
agent_interpretation: Core 15E slice is already shipped.
Revisit this area only when later LLVM, daemon, or profiling work exposes new optimization opportunities.
Do not treat 15E as the primary next roadmap item ahead of 15D, 15F, 15G, or 15H.

phases.label: Phase 15F
phases.title: Phase 15F: Incremental Daemon, Background Prewarm, and Parallel Compilation
status_notes: Why it matters now: it is the next major untouched execution slice after 15D
goal: Keep parsed, typed, effect-annotated, and lowered state warm across edits.
Prewarm caches and workspace indexes in the background.
Parallelize independent frontend and backend work without breaking deterministic diagnostics.
main_motive: Make Agam development loops feel instant by keeping compiler state warm and overlapping safe work.
Preserve deterministic diagnostics and invalidation even as the compiler becomes daemonized and parallel.
checklist_status: [ ] Keep parsed, typed, effect-annotated, and lowered state warm across edits in a persistent daemon.
[ ] Prewarm package caches, native caches, and workspace indexes in the background.
[ ] Parallelize independent frontend and backend work with deterministic diagnostics and fine-grained invalidation.
planned_work: Keep parsed, typed, effect-annotated, and lowered state warm across edits in a persistent daemon.
Prewarm package caches, native caches, and workspace indexes in the background so startup and rerun latency feel instant.
Parallelize independent frontend and backend work while preserving deterministic diagnostics and fine-grained invalidation.
strategic_next_step: Keep typed and lowered state warm across edits.
Parallelize independent compiler work with deterministic diagnostics.
what_to_build_next: Design and ship the persistent daemon surface.
Add background prewarm for package/native/workspace state.
Add deterministic parallel compilation with fine-grained invalidation.
dependencies_and_prerequisites: package/cache/runtime data already emitted by 15A and 15B
deterministic diagnostic ownership across frontend and backend work
agent_interpretation: This is the next major net-new systems slice once the highest-priority remaining 15D work is in hand.
The daemon/workspace contract should be designed to support 15G rather than becoming a separate side system.

phases.label: Phase 15G
phases.title: Phase 15G: First-Party Premium Experience Layer
status_notes: Why it matters now: several visible workflow surfaces are already shipped, but the unification work is still open
goal: Make Agam tooling feel like one coherent first-party product rather than a set of unrelated commands.
Unify workspace conventions, manifests, cache metadata, package/runtime metadata, tooling, and the future daemon behind one stable contract.
main_motive: Make Agam feel like one premium first-party environment instead of many loosely coordinated tools.
Keep manifests, cache layout, diagnostics, package/runtime metadata, and editor/test workflows under one contract.
checklist_status: [x] Ship `agamc doctor` as the first native backend / SDK self-diagnosis command.
[x] Ship one-command workflows such as `agamc dev`, `agamc new`, and cache/status introspection.
[ ] Unify `agam_lsp`, `agam_fmt`, `agam_test`, package/runtime metadata, and the daemon behind one stable workspace contract.
[x] Define strict first-party conventions for manifests, templates, diagnostics, and project layout so Agam gets Apple-like ecosystem coherence.
already_done: Shipped `agamc doctor` as the first native backend and SDK readiness command.
Added `agamc new`, `agamc dev`, and `agamc cache status`.
Defined the first explicit first-party workspace contract around `agam.toml`, `src/main.agam`, `tests/*.agam`, and `.agam_cache/`.

Added a structured benchmark workspace under `benchmarks/` with categorized suites, harnesses, CI helpers, result roots, and methodology docs, while keeping `.agent/test/` as the localized phase-work microbenchmark area.

Expanded the benchmark workspace with explicit Win11/Linux/WSL environment profiles, Agam LLVM/JIT/C target variants, call-cache on/off comparisons, Clang++/Clang/Rust/CPython/Go comparison targets, and richer space-profile reporting for SSD, RAM, cache capacity, and register-budget context.

Tightened benchmark fairness by running Agam through the built driver binary instead of `cargo run`, compiling emitted C backend output to a native executable before runtime measurement, and adding a compile warmup pass before measured AOT compile timing.

Published the first same-host README benchmark snapshot and plot set for the `01_algorithms/fibonacci` comparison matrix so public docs show a concrete Win11 run instead of just the benchmark scaffold.
Added a reproducible benchmark scorecard output plus README winner summary so the public benchmark section can rank targets and explain the current snapshot without hiding the underlying raw metrics.
remaining_work: Unify `agam_lsp`, `agam_fmt`, `agam_test`, package/runtime metadata, and the future daemon behind the same stable workspace contract instead of per-tool discovery.
strategic_next_step: Build on `agamc dev`, `agamc new`, and `agamc cache status`.
Unify package, runtime, cache, and tooling conventions behind the same first-party manifest and project layout.
what_to_build_next: Unify `agam_lsp`, `agam_fmt`, `agam_test`, package/runtime metadata, and daemon expectations behind one stable workspace contract.
Reduce per-tool discovery drift and ad hoc layout assumptions.
agent_interpretation: Treat 15G as a product-integration phase, not a command-count phase.
The next useful work should reduce drift between tooling surfaces and prepare for the daemon from 15F.

phases.label: Phase 15H
phases.title: Phase 15H: Native LLVM SDK Distribution and Toolchain Bundles
status_notes: Why it matters now: the local SDK flow exists, but hosted-runner validation and Android target-pack work remain open
goal: Ship a host-native Agam SDK layout with bundled LLVM support and an explicit readiness contract.
Keep `agamc package sdk`, bundled toolchain discovery, and `agamc doctor` aligned.
main_motive: Ship Agam with a real native SDK and bundled LLVM story instead of setup instructions scattered across docs.
Make toolchain readiness inspectable and distributable across Windows, Linux, and later Android packs.
checklist_status: [x] Add `agamc package sdk` to stage a host-native compiler SDK layout under `dist/<host>`.
[x] Define the SDK layout contract around `bin/agamc`, `toolchains/llvm`, and `sdk-manifest.json`.
[x] Support bundled LLVM discovery next to `agamc` plus explicit bundle roots through `AGAM_LLVM_BUNDLE_DIR`.
[x] Validate local Windows SDK packaging with a real bundled LLVM payload and a staged-SDK self-host smoke run.
[x] Add a CI workflow skeleton for Windows and Linux SDK packaging around the staged SDK layout.
[ ] Validate the Windows and Linux SDK workflow on hosted runners with real bundled LLVM payloads.
[ ] Add Android target-pack packaging and validation on top of the host SDK flow.
already_done: Added `agamc package sdk` to stage a host-native SDK rooted at `dist/<host>`.
Implemented the bundled LLVM contract under `toolchains/llvm/<platform>/bin`.
Added `sdk-manifest.json` to record host and target support.
Added `scripts/package_sdk.py` as the local and CI packaging wrapper.
Added `.github/workflows/sdk-dist.yml` as the first Windows/Linux SDK packaging workflow.
Validated the Windows host flow locally with a real official LLVM install and a staged self-host smoke build.
Added `agamc doctor` integration so SDK distributions can report bundled LLVM, Android sysroot, and native driver readiness.
remaining_work: Validate the workflow on hosted runners and publish real Windows/Linux SDK artifacts with bundled LLVM toolchains.
Add Android target-pack packaging, sysroot validation, and smoke builds.
Add machine-readable `agamc doctor` output for CI and installer gating once the text contract settles.
strategic_next_step: Keep `agamc package sdk` and `agamc doctor` aligned around one SDK readiness contract.
Produce CI-built Windows and Linux SDK packs with bundled LLVM.
Add Android target-pack packaging and validation on top of the host SDK flow.
what_to_build_next: Validate Windows/Linux hosted-runner workflows with real bundled LLVM payloads.
Add Android target-pack packaging, sysroot validation, and smoke builds.
Add machine-readable `agamc doctor` output once the text contract settles.
dependencies_and_prerequisites: native LLVM discovery and bundled-LLVM contract
CI packaging wrapper and workflow skeleton
agent_interpretation: Treat 15H as a productization and distribution phase, not just a packaging script phase.
Prefer real hosted-runner proof and Android target-pack validation over more local-only polish.

phases.label: Phase 16
phases.title: Phase 16: Interactive REPL and Sandboxed Execution
goal: Build an interactive REPL and a headless execution surface for Agam strings.
main_motive: Let users and agent systems evaluate Agam interactively without forcing a full build loop every time.
Provide a clean execution surface for notebooks, exploration, and future LLM-facing runtime services.
planned_work: Build `agam_notebook` and `agam_jit` into a REPL for instant tensor and data-science evaluation without full build steps.
Build a headless API sandbox server endpoint that accepts Agam strings, evaluates them through the JIT, and returns strict JSON `stdout` and `stderr`.
what_to_build_next: Build the core REPL loop.
Define the strict JSON contract for headless execution.
Keep the surface compatible with later agent-execution work in Phase 18.
agent_interpretation: This is a user-facing execution-product phase and should build on the daemon/workspace contract from 15F and 15G, not bypass them.

phases.label: Phase 17
phases.title: Phase 17: Package Ecosystem, Registry, and Reproducible Environments
status_notes: Current note: Phase 17A contract groundwork is now started in `agam_pkg`
goal: Build Agam into a coherent package ecosystem with a stable source manifest, deterministic resolution, registry protocol, reproducible environments, and curated first-party distributions.
Keep portable runtime packages, SDK/toolchain packs, source dependencies, and local environments as separate contracts instead of one overloaded format.
main_motive: Stop package, SDK, workspace, and environment concerns from collapsing into one messy tool story.
Make Agam reproducible across teams and compute environments without copying Cargo, pip, or Conda blindly.
planned_work: Split the package ecosystem into subphases 17A through 17F so manifest, lockfile, registry, environment, official-distribution, and standard-library work can advance without being conflated.
Preserve the existing Phase 15A portable package/runtime contract and Phase 15H SDK distribution contract instead of reopening them casually.
Build the future package manager on content-addressable, reproducible metadata rather than ad hoc install scripts or repo-name coupling.
already_done: Added the first `agam_pkg` source-workspace contract types for `agam.toml`, `agam.lock`, dependency specs, toolchain requirements, and named environments.
Added TOML read/write helpers and round-trip tests for the new workspace and lockfile data models.
Wired `agam_driver` project scaffolding and workspace resolution through the new manifest contract instead of hand-rendering `agam.toml`.
Added first-party manifest entry-path validation so `project.entry` must stay inside the workspace root.
remaining_work: Reuse the parsed source-workspace contract across formatter, tests, LSP, and later daemon surfaces instead of letting each tool rediscover layout independently.
Keep Phase 17A moving until the manifest contract is stable enough for registry and environment UX on top.
what_to_build_next: Start with 17A and freeze the workspace/package manifest surface.
Then land 17B deterministic resolution and lockfile behavior before building registry or environment UX.
Treat 17C through 17E as product layers built on top of those contracts.
Continue the standard-library and native I/O growth in 17F after the package layers stop drifting.
agent_interpretation: Do not mix source dependencies, portable runtime packages, SDK packs, and environments into one format.
The highest-leverage near-term package work is contract definition and reproducibility, not wrapper count or installer sprawl.

phases.label: Phase 17A
phases.title: Phase 17A: Workspace Contract and Dependency Manifests
status_notes: Current note: `agam_pkg` now carries the first source-workspace, dependency, lockfile, and environment data models, and `agam_driver` consumes the manifest contract for project scaffolding and workspace resolution
goal: Define one stable `agam.toml` contract for package metadata, workspace layout, dependency declarations, and toolchain expectations.
Separate source-package metadata from portable runtime package artifacts and SDK distribution manifests.
main_motive: Stop each tool from inventing its own package and workspace assumptions.
Make package identity, dependency declaration, and project layout deterministic before a resolver or registry is built on top.
checklist_status: [x] Add the first `agam_pkg` data models for source manifests, dependency specs, lockfiles, and environments.
[x] Wire the manifest contract into `agamc new` and workspace discovery.
[x] Validate `project.entry` so the manifest cannot point outside the workspace root.
[ ] Freeze the manifest naming and compatibility policy before building resolver flows on top.
[ ] Strengthen first-party diagnostics for malformed dependency, environment, and workspace-member metadata.
planned_work: Define the first stable source-package manifest schema for `agam.toml`.
Add package identity, version, workspace members, dependency sections, target intent, and toolchain requirement fields.
Keep the manifest aligned with the existing first-party layout from Phase 15G instead of creating a second project model.
Define diagnostics and validation rules so malformed manifests fail clearly through first-party tooling.
already_done: Added `WorkspaceManifest`, `DependencySpec`, `ToolchainRequirement`, `EnvironmentSpec`, and `WorkspaceLockfile` in `agam_pkg`.
Added TOML read/write helpers plus round-trip tests for manifest and lockfile compatibility, including the current `[project]` manifest shape.
Switched `agamc new` to scaffold `agam.toml` through `agam_pkg::scaffold_workspace_manifest` and `agam_pkg::write_workspace_manifest_to_path`.
Switched workspace resolution in `agam_driver` to parse `agam.toml`, honor `project.entry`, expose the manifest project name, and reject entry paths that escape the workspace root.
remaining_work: Reuse the parsed manifest contract across formatter, tests, LSP, and later daemon surfaces.
Define stronger user-facing diagnostics around malformed dependency, environment, and workspace-member metadata.
what_to_build_next: Freeze the manifest shape and naming conventions first.
Cover workspace members and local path dependencies before solving remote resolution.
Reuse the same manifest contract across CLI, formatter, LSP, test, and future daemon surfaces.
agent_interpretation: This is the foundation package phase.
Do not add resolver, registry, or environment behavior until the manifest contract stops moving.

phases.label: Phase 17B
phases.title: Phase 17B: Deterministic Resolver and Lockfile
goal: Build deterministic dependency resolution and a stable `agam.lock` format for reproducible builds.
Make content addressing and immutable resolution records part of the default workflow.
main_motive: A package ecosystem is not trustworthy until teams can reproduce the same dependency graph and tool inputs exactly.
Resolver behavior must be explicit, inspectable, and stable before registry growth scales the problem.
planned_work: Define the `agam.lock` format and the resolved-package record shape.
Support deterministic resolution for workspace, path, git, and registry dependencies.
Use content hashes and release metadata to make package retrieval verifiable.
Keep lockfile behavior aligned with package caches and future daemon prewarm work.
what_to_build_next: Land the lockfile data model first.
Implement reproducible local and git dependency resolution before full registry solving.
Make cache keys, lockfile records, and package identity agree on the same addressing rules.
agent_interpretation: This is the reproducibility phase.
Prefer deterministic behavior and inspectable metadata over clever solver complexity.

phases.label: Phase 17C
phases.title: Phase 17C: Registry Index and Publish Protocol
goal: Define the central Agam registry-index protocol and the immutable publish contract for source packages.
Make package identity registry-based instead of coupling it to Git repository layout.
main_motive: Agam needs one discoverable ecosystem index, but it should stay thin, auditable, and easy to mirror.
Publishing and installation should be shaped around immutable releases and verified metadata instead of ad hoc cloning.
planned_work: Define a central index repository layout and release-metadata schema.
Add publish, install, update, inspect, and audit flows around immutable package releases.
Support official packages inside the Agam org without forcing every package to live in a separate repository.
Keep the registry protocol simple enough for mirrors and private indexes later.
what_to_build_next: Freeze the index metadata and namespace rules first.
Define publish validation before building UX-heavy install flows.
Keep checksum and provenance data in the registry contract from the start.
agent_interpretation: The registry should be thin and boring by design.
Avoid tying package identity to repo naming conventions or one-repo-per-package assumptions.

phases.label: Phase 17D
phases.title: Phase 17D: Environments and SDK Linking
goal: Build named Agam environments that pin compiler version, SDK packs, target packs, runtime expectations, and dependencies together.
Make per-project environments reproducible without turning Agam into a clone of Conda.
main_motive: Users need a clean way to switch between host-native, Android, data-science, and release environments without manually wiring toolchains each time.
Environment state should be explicit project metadata, not hidden shell-global mutation.
planned_work: Define named environments such as `dev`, `test`, `release`, and target-specific profiles.
Bind environments to compiler versions, SDK packs, target triples, runtime backends, and dependency locks.
Keep environment activation and inspection first-party through Agam tooling.
Align environment metadata with `agamc doctor`, SDK discovery, and future background prewarm work.
what_to_build_next: Start with environment data models and explicit selection rules.
Make SDK and target-pack linkage reproducible before adding shell integration or convenience shortcuts.
Keep environments layered on top of manifests and lockfiles rather than inventing a second resolver.
agent_interpretation: This is an environment-management phase, not a system-package-manager phase.
Interoperate with foreign toolchains, but do not absorb foreign package managers into Agam's core contract.

phases.label: Phase 17E
phases.title: Phase 17E: First-Party Base Distributions and Official Packages
goal: Ship curated first-party base distributions and define how official Agam packages live inside the organization.
Make the default Agam install feel coherent without turning the base tool into a giant all-in-one distribution.
main_motive: Users need an obvious starting point, but Agam should not bundle every SDK, package, and foreign integration by default.
Official package governance has to be clear before the ecosystem grows across many repositories.
planned_work: Define curated first-party package profiles such as base language support, systems/native interop, and data/AI stacks.
Define official package naming, release, and repository governance inside the Agam organization.
Keep foreign-language interop as explicit packs and bridges, not as the base package-manager contract.
Align first-party distributions with the registry and environment model instead of bypassing them.
what_to_build_next: Define the first-party profile taxonomy first.
Then wire official package publication and install flows around that taxonomy.
Keep the default install small and layer larger profiles through explicit environment selection.
agent_interpretation: The goal is first-party coherence, not maximum bundle size.
Curated profiles should reduce ecosystem confusion, not hide real dependency boundaries.

phases.label: Phase 17F
phases.title: Phase 17F: Standard Library and Native I/O Expansion
goal: Expand `agam_std` and native I/O capability in a way that fits the new package ecosystem and Agam's effects model.
Grow standard-library depth without importing a messy async/runtime story from other ecosystems.
main_motive: A strong package ecosystem still needs a credible first-party standard library.
Networking, file I/O, and higher-level batteries should feel language-native, not like thin wrappers around foreign runtime models.
planned_work: Expand `agam_std` so networking, file I/O, and related workflow capability are handled natively through algebraic effects.
Align standard-library packaging and versioning with the new manifest, lockfile, registry, and environment contracts.
Keep the standard library modular enough that first-party base distributions can compose it cleanly.
what_to_build_next: Finish the package ecosystem contract layers first.
Then grow native I/O and networking through the effects model in a way the package manager can distribute reproducibly.
Keep `agam_std` growth aligned with official package governance rather than one-off ad hoc modules.
agent_interpretation: This phase should grow capability without recreating Python-style runtime sprawl.
Standard-library expansion should build on the package ecosystem, not bypass it.

phases.label: Phase 18
phases.title: Phase 18: Build an Agam Execution Tool
goal: Build a secure execution interface so agent frameworks can run Agam code directly.
main_motive: Make Agam usable as a first-class execution language for LLM agents instead of forcing those systems back to Python.
planned_work: Create a secure sandboxed tool where an LLM can input a string of Agam code.
Use the core workflow:
write the code to a temporary `.agam` file
invoke `agamc run`
return `stdout` and `stderr` back to the calling agent system
what_to_build_next: Define the secure execution boundary.
Implement the temp-file plus `agamc run` loop.
Keep outputs strict and agent-consumable.
agent_interpretation: This is the first direct bridge from Agam to external agent workflows.

phases.label: Phase 19
phases.title: Phase 19: LangChain and LlamaIndex Wrappers
goal: Make Agam easy to adopt in popular AI-agent ecosystems.
main_motive: Reduce adoption friction by making Agam feel like a drop-in execution upgrade for existing Python-based agent stacks.
planned_work: Build `AgamREPLTool` as a native Python class.
Publish it as a drop-in replacement for `PythonREPLTool` in LangChain and LlamaIndex.
Extend `agam_ffi` so existing Python frameworks can seamlessly call compiled native Agam.
what_to_build_next: Build the first `AgamREPLTool`.
Package the LangChain/LlamaIndex integration story.
Strengthen Python-facing FFI for native Agam execution.
agent_interpretation: This phase is ecosystem adoption work and should follow a strong Phase 18 execution tool surface.

phases.label: Phase 20
phases.title: Phase 20: Tree-Sitter and Prompt Engineering
goal: Improve model reliability when generating Agam code.
main_motive: Prevent agent systems from hallucinating Python or generic syntax when they are supposed to produce Agam.
planned_work: Write a formal Tree-Sitter grammar for Agam to constrain token generation.
Add context-injection system prompts such as "You are an expert in Agam. Do not use Python."
Build a few-shot context serializer that can automatically inject 5-10 high-quality Agam syntax examples, especially for ML-heavy syntax such as `tensor` and `grad`.
what_to_build_next: Ship the Tree-Sitter grammar.
Formalize the anti-Python prompt guidance.
Build the reusable few-shot example injector.
dependencies_and_prerequisites: enough stable syntax examples and parser shape
agent_interpretation: This is one of the key anti-hallucination phases for agentic Agam use.

phases.label: Phase 21
phases.title: Phase 21: Model Fine-Tuning
goal: Train models that natively reason in Agam syntax.
main_motive: Move from prompt-level syntax steering to models that can actually think in Agam.
planned_work: Build a dataset of thousands of `.agam` scripts plus expected outputs.
Fine-tune an open-source model such as Llama or Mistral so it can produce Agam without heavy prompt engineering.
what_to_build_next: Build the dataset first.
Then evaluate a fine-tuning path on an open model.
dependencies_and_prerequisites: execution and validation surfaces from earlier agent phases
agent_interpretation: This is a long-term data and model phase, not an immediate compiler-core priority.

phases.label: Phase 22
phases.title: Phase 22: Omni-Targeting Directives
goal: Make Agam target radically different deployment environments through language-level target directives.
main_motive: Make Agam truly ubiquitous instead of a desktop-only or server-only language.
Scale from tiny bare-metal deployments to heavy enterprise and HPC targets through explicit target semantics.
planned_work: Implement `@target.iot` so the compiler can strip `agam_std`, strip ARC memory, refuse heap allocations, and target sub-10KB static binaries for devices such as ESP32 and Arduino.
Implement `@target.enterprise` to enable heavier runtime services such as GC choice, thread pooling, and capability-sandbox locking.
Implement `@target.hpc` so `agam_codegen` and `agam_mir` can prefer aggressive vectorization and compute-oriented behavior.
what_to_build_next: Design the semantic meaning of the target directives.
Then wire the target-specific runtime/codegen restrictions and preferences.
dependencies_and_prerequisites: clear runtime contract and target-pack direction
agent_interpretation: This phase should build on the current LLVM and SDK platform work, not run ahead of it.

phases.label: Phase 23
phases.title: Phase 23: GPU and NPU Integration
goal: Extend Agam into direct GPU and accelerator execution.
main_motive: Let Agam target serious ML and accelerator hardware directly instead of routing everything through C++ wrappers and external frameworks.
planned_work: Add an `@gpu` or `#[gpu(threads=512)]` kernel macro surface for native ML and compute kernels.
Transition `agam_codegen` and LLVM emission toward `nvptx64` / NVPTX for CUDA and `amdgcn` / AMDGPU for ROCm.
Add MLIR-oriented lowering and dialect planning for NPUs such as Apple Neural Engine and Qualcomm Hexagon.
what_to_build_next: Define the kernel macro surface.
Extend LLVM/codegen toward CUDA NVPTX and AMDGPU ROCm targets.
Design MLIR/NPU lowering paths after the core GPU story is credible.
agent_interpretation: This is a major backend expansion phase and depends on a mature LLVM/toolchain foundation.

phases.label: Phase 24
phases.title: Phase 24: Advanced DSA and Scientific Computing
goal: Make Agam's native scientific and data-structure surface competitive and broad.
main_motive: Ensure Agam is not just a compiler demo, but a language with native scientific-computing and performance-data-structure depth.
planned_work: Implement sparse matrix hardware-aware types for large NLP and graph workloads.
Build native FFT and signal-processing support inside `agam_std::math`.
Build a high-performance `agam_std::collections` with lock-free and vectorized structures such as B-Trees, graphs, and fast maps.
what_to_build_next: Expand sparse and numerical primitives first.
Then push the high-performance collections story.
agent_interpretation: This phase grows the native standard library and should stay performance-grounded.

phases.label: Phase 25
phases.title: Phase 25: Machine-Code Control and Optimizations
goal: Expose lower-level control surfaces for systems and performance work.
main_motive: Give systems and kernel-class users exact machine-level control where Agam needs to compete seriously.
Turn existing PGO plumbing into a full guided optimization loop rather than a partial flag surface.
planned_work: Add inline assembly parsing and support such as `asm! { "mov eax, 1" }` for direct silicon and kernel-facing control.
Extend the existing `--pgo-generate` and `--pgo-use` plumbing into a full production-quality PGO loop that can collect, merge, and reuse profiles.
what_to_build_next: Complete the PGO loop into a real workflow.
Add inline assembly only with strong syntax and safety boundaries.
agent_interpretation: This phase should stay tightly aligned with safety and proof quality rather than becoming a raw-escape hatch everywhere.

phases.label: Phase 26
phases.title: Phase 26: Quantum and ZKP Primitives
goal: Explore frontier language-native support for quantum and zero-knowledge-proof primitives.
main_motive: Push Agam into future-facing computation domains without reducing those domains to library-only afterthoughts.
planned_work: Add native `Qubit` types and quantum gate operations.
Add `#[zkp]`-style compilation support so mathematical logic can lower directly into zero-knowledge-proof workflows.
what_to_build_next: Treat this as a long-horizon research phase.
Do not prioritize it ahead of the product-path compiler/runtime work.
dependencies_and_prerequisites: strong core language and IR foundations
agent_interpretation: This is a far-future frontier phase, not a near-term product priority.

phases.label: Phase 27
phases.title: Phase 27: Premium Object System
goal: Finish the existing struct/class/trait/impl surface into a coherent premium object model.
main_motive: Complete Agam's object-system story so object-oriented code feels language-native instead of half-parsed or bolted on.
planned_work: Finish the existing `struct` / `class` / `trait` / `impl` parser surface into end-to-end semantics.
Add constructors, `self`, visibility rules, and premium method dispatch.
Decide Agam's long-term composition, inheritance, and interface ergonomics.
what_to_build_next: Define the full object model contract.
Then implement constructors, `self`, visibility, and dispatch coherently across the compiler pipeline.
agent_interpretation: This phase should prioritize language coherence over feature-count inflation.

phases.label: Phase 28
phases.title: Phase 28: Premium Ergonomics and Syntax Cohesion
goal: Make everyday Agam syntax and APIs feel premium, coherent, and low-ceremony.
main_motive: Make Agam feel easy like Python while staying organized like serious systems languages.
Align parser, formatter, and LSP around one canonical ergonomic syntax profile.
planned_work: Add named arguments and default parameter values so high-arity calls stay readable without overload explosion.
Reduce declaration ceremony further where safety does not depend on the extra syntax.
Unify constructor, property, and method organization across script-style code and object-style APIs.
Feed those ergonomics rules back into `agam_fmt` and `agam_lsp` so the language not only parses the syntax, but teaches and preserves the premium style.
what_to_build_next: Define the canonical ergonomic syntax profile.
Implement named/default arguments and lower-ceremony syntax in a way formatter and LSP can preserve.
dependencies_and_prerequisites: parser and sema support for the new surface
agent_interpretation: This is a language-polish phase and should reinforce Agam's identity instead of copying other languages blindly.
